items.prompt:
Generate response to the question/instruction based on a piece of given material

Question/Instruction: What are some of the behaviors that educators have reported as increasing in Oregon classrooms?

Given material: The Oregon Education Association released a report Tuesday detailing issues in classrooms that disrupt learning and solutions they pose to fix them.
Name calling and profanity. Spitting, kicking and hitting. Ripping books. Brandishing scissors.
These are some of the behaviors educators say are increasing in Oregon classrooms.
Teachers have voiced concerns to the Oregon Education Association over the past few years about disruptive behaviors that not only affect the student doing those things, but also have lasting effects on their peers and educators.
The association released a report Tuesday detailing what they call "a crisis of disruptive learning."
The report delves into these behaviors, what might be causing them and what actions the teachers' union recommends schools, districts, state lawmakers and governmental agencies take to help.
"Now more than ever, we need funding to make sure students get what they need," said John Larson, high school English teacher and president of the Oregon Education Association.
"We also have to realize today ... We have many students with unmet needs," he said. "Housing insecurity, food insecurity, unmet mental-health needs. They have needs in- and outside of school that many of our members are not equipped to deal to with."
School district administrators and Oregon Department of Education staff had not seen the report Monday and could not comment in time for publication.
"While we have not had the opportunity to review the findings, we are committed to building social and emotional learning in Salem-Keizer Public Schools," said Lillian Govus, director of communications for the district.
"By truly understanding what is driving students' behaviors — not just in Salem-Keizer but across the region — we can develop appropriate responses that minimize disruptions and maintain safe educational spaces for our educators and our children," she said.
The report was released just two weeks into Oregon's 2019 legislative session.
Lawmakers are expected to have a large focus on education, especially since the Joint Committee on Student Success is set to develop a comprehensive budget and policy road map to improve K-12 schools.
Colt Gill, director of the Oregon Department of Education, attended the meeting at South Salem High School in November and said there may be legislative opportunities to make these changes happen.
Larson said the association will make sure every legislator has a copy of this report.
While the association estimates another $4 billion over the current service level is needed per biennium to get pre-K through higher education where they need to be, Larson recognized it has to be a long-term, sustainable investment and it won't happen immediately.
The report includes feedback from from hundreds of educators around Oregon. The association, in partnership with the Oregon Department of Education, hosted 14 forums and heard from hundreds of participants online, officials said.
"Oregon is filled with quality educators, quality support professionals who care about every student," he said. "But the conditions in our school make it nearly impossible for them to do their jobs successful.
"We need to make sure we aren't selling our future short."
According to the report, a disruptive learning environment is defined when student behavior significantly interferes with instruction and/or school staff members' ability to maintain a stable classroom or ensure student safety.
At times, these behaviors can become dangerous to the student exhibiting them, or the classroom as a whole.
The report lists myriad negative behaviors educators see regularly — verbal abuse, person-to-person abuse, weaponizing the classroom and destruction of property.
At listening sessions across the state, educators told the association about the "atmosphere of violence that was palpable," of attacks that came regularly and of trauma students experience in that environment.
And while these behaviors may not all be new, educators have reported an increase in the severity and frequency in recent years.
School districts are not required to keep formal data on incidents like "room clears."
A room clear is when students temporarily leave a classroom to reduce the likelihood of injury or trauma as a result of extreme behavior. This typically happens when one student is exhibiting behavior that puts the rest of the class at risk.
The association surveyed educators and students across Oregon on these issues.
Of the respondents, 32 percent said they were scared for students' safety at school, 25 percent said they were concerned for their own safety and 56 percent said their classroom or their child's classroom had experienced at least one room clear this year.
Anecdotally though, many families, administrators and staff have reported to the association that weekly or daily room clears have become commonplace, according to the report.
And according to the bi-annual poll called the Oregon Teaching, Empowering, Leading and Learning survey, educators feel less safe now than they did in 2014, with the percentage of staff who agreed the environment they work in is safe dropping from 92 percent in 2014 to 82 percent in 2018.
"There is not a reliable, timely or effective system in place to intervene when there are consistent disruptions," one respondent told the association. "It feels like a Band-Aid approach."
These behaviors can affect everyone in the classroom.
The student exhibiting the behaviors often lose instructional time and needed supports, and educators said they can experience social isolation and emotional trauma as a result of how peers treat them in the aftermath of the events.
The students witnessing or being harmed by the behaviors also lose out on instructional time, when Oregon already has one of the shortest school years in the country.
Some of these peers have been physically injured. According to the report, others have a hard time learning even when an event isn't occurring due to the stress or fear that something will.
Educators told the association they feel discouraged "that often the only intervention available is for students to be removed from class and 'babysat' in an office, rather than addressing their unmet needs or receive tools to manage their behavior."
"Quite honestly, this is a community problem," Larson said. "This is something that we really need to solve together.
"Our students are at risk," he said. "We need to make sure they are safe and able to learn."
When asked "Why do disrupted learning environments occur?", one participant wrote, "More problems and less support."
The association said decades of "chronic and systemic disinvestment in Oregon schools" is perhaps the most influential reason for this, pointing to the fact that, over the last two decades, the Legislature has consistently financed schools at about 21 to 38 percent below what its own research suggested districts needed to be successful.
However, the report also lists a number of other contributing factors, including increased class sizes; decreased student support specialists; students with high needs but no resources to support them; a marked decreased in "well-rounded education opportunities" and time for physical activity; a lack of appropriate training for educators and an "unclear implementation of policies regarding restraint and seclusion, k-5 expulsion and suspension" and protocols for disruptions.
Not every district is affected by all of these factors, though.
For example, large class sizes are a significant issue in districts like Salem-Keizer Public Schools, which recently passed a capital-construction bond and is planning to change boundaries to address overcrowding.
However, smaller, rural districts aren't usually battling large classes. Instead, they may be facing increased percentages of students who are living in poverty and experiencing homelessness or food insecurity.
And even within one school and one classroom, every student will have their own background and experiences.
"It's different for every, single child," Larson said, adding that's why the state needs more professional development to help better prepare staff. He said additional adults in the classroom and, in some cases, improved curriculum are also needed.
Larson said the goal is to get the resources so each school can look at its population and ask what students need to get the best education possible.
The Oregon Education Association's report includes recommendations that are meant to address the short- and long-term needs in classrooms statewide.
Three key ideas were prioritized — increasing onsite student supports with a focus on mental health, reducing class size and caseloads, and fully funding targeted professional development and ongoing supports for implementation.
Each recommendation includes steps for schools, districts and the state to take.
For onsite supports, the association recommends more mental health counselors, school psychologists, social workers, school nurses, speech language pathologists, occupational and physical therapists and other mental health and trauma experts.
Concerning class size, the state's most recent class size report by the Oregon Department of Education documented 579 classes with 56 or more students.
Additionally, almost half of classes are 35 to 51 percent larger than research recommends, according to the department's report.
Oregon Gov. Kate Brown has recommended paying for additional staff in order to reduce class sizes, especially in K-3 classes, where students are not only learning academic basics, but social norms and appropriate behaviors as well.
And for professional development, the association recommends ongoing training and support for staff to "fully transfer new knowledge into practice."
"We have inadequate training for new curriculum," one teacher told the association. "Teachers in my school are expected to be doing restorative practices/circles regularly in our classrooms; however, not one hour of training has been given so that teachers do this in a truly inclusive and helpful way."
The association recommends staff getting thorough and consistent training on models of trauma-informed practices, social and emotional learning programs and positive behavior programs, to name a few.
Though the report focuses on those three key recommendations, it also includes a number of other ideas that would reportedly help improve safety in schools.
Family wraparound services, increased physical movement and clarified protocols for physical intervention and restraint of students are just a few.
For more information, go to https://www.oregoned.org/ or contact the Oregon Education Association at 503-684-3300.
items.completion:
Educators in Oregon have reported several behaviors that are increasing in classrooms, including:

1. **Verbal and Physical Abuse**: Such as name calling, profanity, spitting, kicking, hitting, and other forms of verbal and physical aggression.

2. **Destruction of Property**: Actions like ripping books, brandishing scissors, and other acts that disrupt the learning environment.

3. **Weaponization of the Classroom**: Using classroom items as weapons, which can pose a direct threat to students and staff.

4. **Dangerous Behavior**: Behaviors that can put students or staff in harm's way, such as acts that are both physically and emotionally threatening.

5. **Increased Frequency and Severity of Disruptions**: Educators report that these behaviors are occurring more frequently and with greater intensity over the past few years.

6. **Room Clears**: Students are often removed from the classroom temporarily (room clears) to prevent harm or trauma, which is becoming more common.

7. **Fear and Safety Concerns**: Many educators and students express fear for their safety, with 32% of respondents indicating they are scared for students' safety, and 25% for their own safety.

8. **Systemic Issues**: The report highlights a lack of reliable, timely, and effective interventions, as well as chronic underfunding and systemic disinvestment in Oregon schools.

These behaviors are not only affecting students' learning but also impacting educators' well-being, safety, and ability to teach effectively. The Oregon Education Association emphasizes the need for systemic changes, including increased mental health supports, reduced class sizes, and adequate funding for professional development and school resources.

items.prompt: Write a step-by-step tutorial on how to create the perfect at-home spa day.
items.completion:
Creating the perfect at-home spa day is a rewarding way to relax, rejuvenate, and treat yourself. Below is a step-by-step tutorial to help you craft a luxurious and relaxing at-home spa experience:

---

### **Step 1: Plan Your Spa Day**
- **Set a Date and Time:** Choose a day when you’re fully rested and can enjoy the experience without interruptions. Consider weekends or evenings.
- **Define Your Goals:** Decide what you want to achieve—relaxation, skincare, stress relief, or even a full-body massage. This will guide your choices.
- **Create a Schedule:** Allocate specific times for different activities (e.g., skincare, massage, relaxation, and leisure).

---

### **Step 2: Gather Your Spa Supplies**
- **Essential Products:** 
  - Skincare products (toner, serum, moisturizer, face mask).
  - Body scrub or exfoliant.
  - Massage tools (massager, foam roller,精油).
  - Aromatherapy diffuser or essential oil diffuser.
  - Soft towels, cotton balls, and a cozy blanket.
- **Decorations:** Add calming décor like soft lighting, candles, or a small plant.
- **Comfort Items:** Comfortable seating, a cozy blanket, and a plush pillow.

---

### **Step 3: Prepare Your Space**
- **Clean the Area:** Ensure the room is clean and free of clutter. Use a diffuser or scented candles to create a calming atmosphere.
- **Adjust Lighting:** Use soft, warm lighting to create a relaxing ambiance.
- **Set the Mood:** Play soothing music or use a sound machine to enhance the relaxing experience.

---

### **Step 4: Begin with a Skincare Routine**
- **Wash Your Face:** Start with a gentle cleanser to remove impurities.
- **Apply Skincare Products:** Follow a routine that includes toner, serum, moisturizer, and a face mask if desired.
- **Exfoliate (Optional):** Use a body scrub or exfoliant to remove dead skin cells and refresh your skin.

---

### **Step 5: Indulge in a Massage or Self-Myotherapy**
- **Choose a Massage Type:** Options include Swedish massage, deep tissue, hot stone, or a custom session.
- **Use a Massage Tool:** If you don’t have a professional, use a foam roller or a massager to target specific areas.
- **Focus on Tension Points:** Pay attention to areas of tension, such as shoulders, back, or feet.

---

### **Step 6: Incorporate Aromatherapy**
- **Add Essential Oils:** Use lavender, eucalyptus, or peppermint to enhance relaxation and improve mood.
- **Use a Diffuser:** Run the diffuser for 10–15 minutes to release calming scents throughout the room.

---

### **Step 7: Engage in Relaxing Activities**
- **Read a Book or Watch a Movie:** Choose something calming and enjoyable.
- **Practice Mindfulness or Meditation:** Use guided sessions or apps to help you stay present.
- **Enjoy a Warm Bath:** Add Epsom salts, a bath bomb, and a few drops of essential oil for a luxurious soak.

---

### **Step 8: Wrap Up with a Final Relaxation**
- **Rest and Recover:** Allow yourself to fully unwind. Take a few deep breaths and enjoy the moment.
- **Reflect on the Experience:** Take a moment to appreciate your efforts and the peace you’ve created.

---

### **Step 9: Clean Up and Store Supplies**
- **Wipe Down Surfaces:** Clean any used products or tools.
- **Store Items Properly:** Return items to their designated places to keep your space tidy.

---

By following these steps, you’ll create a serene and rejuvenating at-home spa day that feels like a luxury escape. The key is to be mindful of your needs, enjoy the process, and prioritize self-care. 🌿✨

items.prompt: Write a 5000-word thriller story in third person narrative about a person who's stranded on a deserted island, incorporating elements of survival, danger, and mystery. The story should contain intense descriptions of the protagonist's emotional state and physical struggle, as well as unpredictable twists and turns that keep the reader on edge. The protagonist must face unexpected threats and obstacles, including potential encounters with dangerous wild animals or hostile human beings. Additionally, the story should include moments of hope, despair, and unexpected surprises that challenge the protagonist's will to survive. The ending should be satisfying and provide closure for the reader, while still leaving some questions open-ended for interpretive purposes. The story should be written in an engaging and suspenseful style, with well-developed characters and a well-paced plot.
items.completion:
**Title: The Last Survivor**

---

### **Chapter 1: The Descent**

The storm had been a cruel joke. It had come without warning, a dark, unrelenting force that swallowed the sky in a shroud of clouds. The wind howled like a beast, tearing at the sails of the *Aurora*, a small fishing boat that had once been a symbol of hope for the crew. The crew had been gone for days, lost in the chaos of the sea, their fate uncertain. But the storm had not been a mere accident—it was a test, a trial, and the only way to find the truth.

I was the last one on the boat. I had been the only one left, the only one who had not succumbed to the storm’s wrath. My name is Elias Voss, and I was stranded on this desolate island, a place where the sea had claimed its victims and the land had become a graveyard of lost souls.

The island was not what I expected. It was not the lush, tropical paradise I had imagined. It was a jagged, windswept expanse of stone and sand, where the air was thick with the scent of salt and decay. The trees were skeletal, their branches twisted and gnarled, and the ground was cracked and uneven, as if the earth itself had been torn apart by the force of the storm. It was a place of silence, a place where the only sound was the whisper of the wind and the distant cry of a distant bird.

I had been thrown off the boat when the storm hit. The boat had capsized, and I had been swept into the sea, my body aching with the pain of the fall. I had survived, but not without a price. My legs were broken, my lungs burned, and my mind was a battlefield of fear and determination. I had no shelter, no food, and no sign of any other human being. The island was a prison, and I was its prisoner.

---

### **Chapter 2: The First Days**

The first few days were a blur of survival. I had to find water, and I had to find shelter. The island was not a place of abundance, but it was a place of necessity. I scavenged for anything that could be used—plastic bottles, broken wood, and the occasional fish. I built a small shelter using the wreckage of the boat, a tangle of rope and branches that barely held together against the elements.

The days were long and grueling. I woke up each morning with aching muscles and a mind that was a battlefield of thoughts. I wondered if I would ever find food, if I would ever see the sea again, or if I would be left to die here. The fear of the unknown gnawed at me, but so did the determination to survive.

One morning, I found a small cave on the edge of the island, its entrance hidden by a curtain of vines. I was hesitant, but the thought of shelter was too great. I pushed through the vines, and the cave was a small, dimly lit space, its walls lined with cracks and moss. I used the rope from the boat to climb in, and as I stepped inside, the air was cooler, the scent of damp earth and pine. It was a place of safety, a place where I could rest and think.

But the cave was not without its dangers. The walls were slick with moisture, and the floor was uneven. I had to be careful, but I was determined. I built a small fire in the cave, and the warmth was a comfort. I ate a piece of dried fish, the taste bitter but satisfying, and I lay down, exhausted, as the stars above seemed to watch me.

---

### **Chapter 3: The First Encounter**

The island was not without its dangers. The first real threat came in the form of a wild animal. I had heard tales of the island’s creatures—large, fierce, and unpredictable. I had seen the occasional shadow moving in the distance, but I had dismissed them as figments of my imagination.

One night, I heard a low growl. I was alone in the cave, and the sound was not from the walls or the floor. It was from the darkness beyond the cave. I crept to the edge, my heart pounding, and saw a massive, sleek animal—something like a leopard, but with a body that seemed to defy nature. Its eyes were glowing, and its movements were fluid, almost predatory.

I froze. The creature was not attacking me, but it was watching. I could feel its presence, its weight on the air. I had no choice but to retreat. I ran back to the cave, my heart pounding, my breath ragged. The creature followed me, and I knew I had to be quick. I slipped into the cave, my mind racing. I had no weapon, no fire, and no way to fight back. But I had a plan.

I used the rope from the boat to climb out of the cave, and I ran toward the edge of the island. The creature was close, and I knew I had to get out of there. I ran, my legs burning, my body trembling. The creature was close, and I could feel its presence, but I had to make it to the shore.

I reached the edge, and the creature was there, too. It lunged, but I was too fast. I twisted, and the creature crashed into the sand, its body shaking. I had survived. I was alive. The fear had been overwhelming, but I had not been defeated.

---

### **Chapter 4: The Search for Help**

In the days that followed, I began to think about the possibility of help. I had no idea where I was, but I had a map—a small, torn piece of paper that I had found in the wreckage of the boat. It was a rough sketch of the island, with some landmarks and directions. I had no idea if it was accurate, but I followed the lines with a determined heart.

I traveled along the coast, following the map’s directions, and I found a small village hidden in the jungle. The village was not what I expected. It was a place of mud and shadows, with people who seemed to know nothing of the outside world. They were wary of me, and I was unsure if they would help or not.

I approached them, my heart pounding, and I told them my story. I told them I was stranded, that I had been thrown off the boat, and that I had no idea where I was. They listened, but they did not believe me. They were afraid, and they did not want to help.

I was left with no choice but to rely on my own strength. I scavenged for food, built a fire, and tried to keep myself warm. I learned to read the signs of the island—where the water was, where the food grew, and where the dangers lurked. I was learning, and I was surviving.

But the island was not without its surprises. One day, I found a hidden cave, its entrance hidden by a curtain of vines. I entered, and the cave was unlike anything I had seen before. It was dark, but I could see the flickering light of a small fire. I found a chest, and inside, I found a journal—written by someone who had come before me. The journal was filled with notes, sketches, and stories of survival, of the island, and of the people who had come before.

I read the journal, and it was a revelation. The author had been a survivor, a person who had faced the same dangers I had. They had written about the island, about the creatures, about the people who had come before. I realized that I was not alone. There were others who had come here, and they had survived.

---

### **Chapter 5: The Betrayal**

The journal was a gift, but it was also a trap. The author had written about the island, but they had also hinted at a secret—something that had been hidden. I was not the only one who had come here. There were others, and they had not been found. I was not the only one who had survived.

One night, I found a message hidden in the journal. It was written in a language I did not recognize, but it spoke of a hidden chamber beneath the island, a place where the secrets of the past were kept. I was drawn to it, and I followed the directions, my heart racing. I found the chamber, and inside, I found a map, a set of tools, and a message that spoke of a group of people who had come before me.

But the message was not just a map. It was a warning. The group had been betrayed by the island, and they had been left to die. The message was a call to action, a call to find the truth and to reclaim what had been lost.

I was torn. I had survived, but I had also been betrayed. I had no choice but to follow the message. I left the chamber, my mind racing, and I set out on a journey to find the others. The island was not as dangerous as I had feared, but it was not without its dangers. I was not alone, and I would not be left to face the unknown alone.

---

### **Chapter 6: The Revelation**

The journey was not easy. The island was a labyrinth of dangers, and I was forced to navigate through the jungle, through the storms, and through the shadows of the unknown. I met other survivors, some who had been lost, others who had been betrayed. I learned their stories, their struggles, and their hopes. I was not the only one who had come here, and I was not the only one who had been left to fight for survival.

items.prompt: - The class should have a method for returning the current capacity of the array.
items.completion:
To implement a method that returns the current capacity of an array, we need to consider the following:

1. **Understanding Array Capacity**: The capacity of an array is the maximum number of elements that the array can hold, as defined by the underlying data structure (e.g., a dynamic array or a list).

2. **Implementation Considerations**:
   - If the array is implemented as a dynamic array (e.g., in Java, C#, or Python), the capacity is typically a property that can be accessed directly.
   - If the array is implemented as a list or a similar structure, the capacity may be determined by the underlying data structure.

3. **Example in Different Programming Languages**:
   - **Java**: The `ArrayList` class has a `capacity()` method that returns the current capacity of the list.
   - **Python**: The `list` class does not have a `capacity()` method, but the `len()` function gives the number of elements, and the `__len__()` method can be used to determine the size.
   - **C#**: The `List<T>` class has a `Capacity` property that returns the current capacity of the list.

4. **Method Definition**:
   - For a general class, the method could be defined as `int GetCapacity()`, which returns the current capacity of the array.

**Final Answer**:
The class should have a method `int GetCapacity()` that returns the current capacity of the array. This method can be implemented based on the specific data structure used (e.g., `ArrayList` in Java, `List<T>` in C#, or `len()` in Python).

Some good news? Content can be shaped to speak WITH (not ‘to’) very select groups of people. Knowing your segments means talking to the perfect audience for your particular product or service. Not sure how? ProfileTree can design a content strategy build around results for YOUR business.
How do we do this? By using the methods tested in our own business, and local businesses like yours, to drive results.
So, instead of ‘hoping’ your online activity achieves the result results, content can shape both your traffic and how those potential buyers behave online.
Since, like us, you’re a local SME you’ll know that every investment needs a good reason, a clear view of the right outcomes needed and the right strategy to make it worthwhile.
Northern Ireland companies also ask us to step in to professionalise their digital or content marketing when they’ve outgrown their initial efforts due to some of these factors not being included.
Instead, we’ll press ‘pause’ and take a little time to make sure your content is on a firmer footing.
But what questions should a business ask before starting on a content project?
A small word but a big question. Why is the content being created? Why should the audience react (eg, click through from Google search)? Why should they react to your ‘call to action’ on your website page? Why should they continue to keep in touch through your online presence and channels?
A critical look at your content process from the initial audience research, to their first discovery, to their initial reaction, to their steps on a customer journey should all be viewed from the customer’s view.
A business should constantly ask ‘why’ the customer would react in the way it is hoped and, most of all, examine if the customer is being acted to respond in the way THEY would want to behave or in the way the business would want the customer to behave.
One of the keys to success lies in the subtle difference.
– Again, simple but very powerful. How does the customer convert from your landing page? How do they convert if they aren’t ready to buy…yet? How do they promote your business to others.
Examining the customer journey process and asking ‘how’ the customer moves from stage to stage, bearing in mind different customers will behave in different ways and should have options, can make sure potential buyers have the things they need when they need them.
– It can be tempting to see success as ‘people reached’ or ‘reactions’ or other such metrics. But ultimately measuring the number of customers who have moved along the path you need to support your business and marketing goals forms the ideal measurement of your investment.
You may want to promote more test drives, you may want to invite prospects to an information evening or you may want to boost the numbers of an online community.
Whatever the case may be, starting with your businesses needs and shaping your goals will mean a clear focus on how you want content marketing to work for you and how you’ll measure which elements have worked best and which to adapt. What are some common mistakes businesses make in their content marketing efforts, and how can ProfileTree help companies avoid these pitfalls?
items.completion:
When it comes to content marketing, businesses often fall into common pitfalls that can hinder their effectiveness. Here are some of the most frequent mistakes and how ProfileTree can help avoid them:

### 1. **Lack of Audience Research**  
   - **Mistake:** Businesses may create content without understanding their target audience’s needs, preferences, or behaviors.  
   - **ProfileTree Solution:** ProfileTree conducts in-depth audience research to identify segments, pain points, and preferences. This ensures content is tailored to the right audience, increasing relevance and engagement.

### 2. **Over-Reliance on Comfort Zone Platforms**  
   - **Mistake:** Companies may focus on platforms they already use (e.g., their own website, social media) without exploring other channels where their audience is more active.  
   - **ProfileTree Solution:** ProfileTree helps businesses identify and leverage the right platforms for their audience, ensuring content is delivered where potential customers are most likely to engage.

### 3. **Poor Content Strategy**  
   - **Mistake:** Content is created without a clear strategy, leading to irrelevant or inconsistent messaging.  
   - **ProfileTree Solution:** ProfileTree designs a content strategy that aligns with business goals, ensuring content is purpose-driven, consistent, and aligned with the customer journey.

### 4. **Ineffective Call-to-Action (CTA)**  
   - **Mistake:** CTAs are unclear, not compelling, or not aligned with the customer’s needs.  
   - **ProfileTree Solution:** ProfileTree focuses on creating CTAs that guide users through the customer journey, ensuring they understand the value of the content and how to take action.

### 5. **Ignoring the Customer Journey**  
   - **Mistake:** Businesses may not map out the customer’s journey, leading to content that doesn’t address the right stages or needs.  
   - **ProfileTree Solution:** ProfileTree maps the customer journey, ensuring content is tailored to each stage (e.g., awareness, consideration, decision, loyalty), and provides options for different customer behaviors.

### 6. **Overlooking Measurement and Optimization**  
   - **Mistake:** Businesses focus on metrics like traffic or clicks without measuring the actual impact on conversions or customer behavior.  
   - **ProfileTree Solution:** ProfileTree helps businesses track key performance indicators (KPIs) and continuously optimize content to improve results, ensuring investments are aligned with business goals.

### 7. **Lack of Local Relevance**  
   - **Mistake:** Content is created without considering the local context, leading to a disconnect with the audience.  
   - **ProfileTree Solution:** ProfileTree ensures content is tailored to the local market, leveraging insights from businesses like yours to create relevant and effective strategies.

### 8. **Inconsistent Brand Voice and Messaging**  
   - **Mistake:** Content may not reflect the brand’s voice, leading to confusion or a lack of brand consistency.  
   - **ProfileTree Solution:** ProfileTree helps maintain a consistent brand voice and messaging across all content, reinforcing brand identity and trust.

### 9. **Not Aligning Content with Business Goals**  
   - **Mistake:** Content is created without a clear connection to business objectives, such as increasing sales, leads, or brand awareness.  
   - **ProfileTree Solution:** ProfileTree ensures content is aligned with business goals, focusing on the right outcomes and strategies to drive measurable results.

### 10. **Overlooking the Customer’s Perspective**  
   - **Mistake:** Businesses may not ask “why” the customer is reacting in a certain way, leading to content that doesn’t resonate with the audience.  
   - **ProfileTree Solution:** ProfileTree encourages a customer-centric approach, asking “why” the customer behaves as they do and ensuring content speaks to their needs and motivations.

---

**In summary**, ProfileTree helps businesses avoid these pitfalls by leveraging data-driven insights, tailored strategies, and a customer-centric approach. By focusing on the right audience, platforms, and goals, ProfileTree ensures content marketing efforts are effective, measurable, and aligned with the business’s long-term vision.

Can you summarize the literary work discussed in the text material and explain its themes and symbols?
items.completion:
The text discusses the literary work *The Hunger Games* by Suzanne Collins, focusing on its themes, symbols, and setting. Here's a summary and analysis:

### **Summary**  
*The Hunger Games* follows 16-year-old Katniss Everdeen, who lives in the dystopian district of Panem. After a devastating civil war, the Capitol rules the 12 districts, exploiting them for resources. Katniss, along with her friend Peeta, wins a lottery to participate in the Hunger Games, a televised competition where the top participants are killed. The story unfolds as Katniss navigates the dangers of the Games, her relationships with Peeta, and her struggle to survive and resist the Capitol's oppression.

### **Themes**  
1. **Power and Oppression**: The Capitol's control over the districts highlights the theme of power dynamics. The Capitol's exploitation of the districts and the Games serve as a metaphor for systemic oppression.  
2. **Survival and Resistance**: Katniss's journey emphasizes the resilience of the oppressed. Her ability to survive and resist the Capitol's tyranny reflects the theme of individual resistance against systemic injustice.  
3. **Identity and Self-Worth**: Katniss's growth from a reluctant participant to a confident survivor underscores the theme of self-identity and the importance of standing up for one's values.  
4. **The Cost of Power**: The Games symbolize the cost of power, both in the physical sense (death) and the psychological toll of living in a society that prioritizes control over human dignity.

### **Symbols**  
1. **The Hunger Games**: Represents the spectacle of oppression and the dehumanization of the oppressed. It symbolizes the Capitol's desire to maintain control through fear and spectacle.  
2. **The China Rabbit**: A recurring symbol of gratitude and home. It reflects Katniss's longing for a safe, familiar place and her struggle to find comfort in a world that constantly threatens her.  
3. **The Capitol**: Symbolizes the oppressive regime that exploits the districts for its own gain, highlighting the moral and ethical dilemmas of power.  
4. **The Games**: Symbolize the loss of individuality and the commodification of human life, emphasizing the tragedy of a society that values spectacle over humanity.

### **Conclusion**  
*The Hunger Games* explores profound themes of power, survival, and resistance, using vivid symbolism to critique systemic oppression. The story serves as a commentary on the dangers of authoritarianism and the resilience of the human spirit in the face of adversity.

items.prompt: Write a suspenseful third-person narrative story about a young and inexperienced doctor who finds herself alone and trapped in a hospital after hours with a severely ill patient and a dangerous intruder. As the doctor tries to save her patient's life, she realizes that the intruder is after her and must decide whether to prioritize her own survival or sacrifice herself to save the patient. The story should include vivid descriptions, internal dialogue, and skillful use of pacing and suspense to keep the reader on edge until the very end.
items.completion:
The fluorescent lights of the hospital hummed like a distant, oppressive heartbeat, casting long shadows across the sterile walls. Clara Bennett, a young and inexperienced doctor, had been assigned to a routine shift, but the morning had turned into a nightmare of chaos. She had just finished her last patient, her hands trembling with exhaustion, when the alarm blared—a single, shrill, piercing sound that cut through the silence like a knife. 

She rushed to the control room, her heart pounding, and found the doors locked. The hospital was quiet, save for the faint echo of a patient’s breathing and the distant sound of a heartbeat monitor. But as she pushed the door open, the air was thick with the scent of antiseptic and fear. Inside, the room was dimly lit, the only light coming from a single overhead bulb that flickered unpredictably. 

There, on the bed, was a patient—Aiden Marlow, a man in his late thirties, his face pale, his eyes wide with fear. His breathing was shallow, his pulse weak. Clara’s hands shook as she approached, her mind racing. She had seen countless cases in her training, but this was different. This was a case that could end in a heartbeat. 

But as she reached for the IV drip, a sound shattered the stillness. A muffled crash echoed from the hallway, followed by the unmistakable *thump* of a body hitting the floor. Clara froze. She turned, her breath catching in her throat, and saw a figure emerging from the shadows—tall, bulky, and unmistakably armed. 

“Who’s there?” the intruder shouted, their voice low and menacing. 

Clara’s heart sank. She had no choice. The intruder was a threat, and the patient was in danger. She could save Aiden, but at what cost? 

Her mind raced. She had read about the dangers of hospital security, about the unpredictable nature of intruders. But this was her first time facing one in the middle of the night. She had to act fast. 

She grabbed a syringe from the side table, her fingers steady despite the fear clawing at her. “I’m a doctor,” she called, her voice firm. “I can help you.” 

The intruder stepped closer, their eyes scanning her, assessing her. Clara felt the weight of the moment, the pressure of the seconds that could decide everything. 

“Give me the syringe,” they demanded. 

Clara’s hands shook, but she forced herself to remain calm. “I can’t—” 

“Then you’re a dead man.” The intruder’s voice was cold, a threat that sent a chill down her spine. 

In that instant, Clara made her choice. She reached into her pocket, pulled out a small, folded piece of paper, and handed it to the intruder. “This is my emergency contact. If you’re not satisfied, you can call it. But I won’t let you hurt me.” 

The intruder hesitated, their expression shifting from menace to something else—something like uncertainty. 

With a final glance at Aiden, Clara turned and ran, the sound of her heels pounding against the floor. She didn’t stop to look back. 

The intruder lunged, but Clara was too fast. She ducked, twisted, and managed to escape into the hallway, the door closing behind her with a deafening clang. 

She collapsed against the wall, her breath ragged, her mind a whirlwind of adrenaline and fear. She had saved the patient, but at the cost of her own life. 

As the hours passed, she lay in the hospital’s emergency room, the weight of her decision lingering. She had chosen to sacrifice herself, but in that moment, she had also found a sense of purpose. 

The story of Clara Bennett would be remembered not just for her skill, but for the courage it took to choose between life and death. And in the quiet of the hospital, she knew that her story was just beginning.

items.prompt: What are the main working items of the proposal in the first, second, and third year? Answer according to: Abstract: 研究期間：10208~10307;The planar solid oxide fuel cell (SOFC), a high temperature clean energy device, has recently received much attention due to its compactness, fuel flexibility, and higher energy conversion efficiency. Especially, because of its convenient integration with micro gas turbines for future stationary, high-efficiency, clean electric power generation system, there is an increasing need to develop high-pressure (pressurized-assembly) planar SOFC technology in many advanced developed countries. Just like the gas turbine technology, the high-pressure SOFC technology is also recognized as a country power symbol. This is the motivation of the present proposal. Thus, this proposal focuses on experimental tests and measurements along with numerical simulations and analyses, including three main objectives: (1) To establish an innovative high-pressure SOFC testing platform, and thus a series of high-pressure (1~5 atm) experiments can be performed to understand the high-pressure impact on the cell performance of SOFC stacks; (2) to establish 3D high-pressure electrochemical reaction flow numerical models, and thus predictions of the cell performance of SOFC stacks under high-pressure conditions can be obtained and compared with experimental data; (3) to establish the high-pressure standard cell test procedure and combine these aforementioned experimental and numerical results to evaluate possible difficulties and problems encountered when integrating the high-pressure planar SOFC with micro gas turbine power generation systems. This three-year proposal aims to establish the real testing platform for high-pressure planar SOFC performance measurements. The schematic setup is presented in the figure as shown on the previous page. First year working items: To establish an innovative pressurized-assembly SOFC testing platform and conduct a series of high-pressure (1~5 atm) experiments so that the impact of high pressure conditions on the cell performance of single-cell stacks can be measured. Also, the standard cell testing procedure under high pressure conditions will be established. Second year: Continuing to perform the first year work, we will establish several 3D high-pressure electrochemical reaction flow numerical models, so that predictions of the cell performance of SOFC stacks under high-pressure conditions can be obtained and compared with experimental data. Third year: 第三年度：Keeping the second year work going, we will investigate micro ion transport phenomena in single-cell stack under high pressure conditions. Finally, using these aforementioned experimental and numerical results, we will evaluate possible difficulties and problems encountered when integrating the high-pressure planar SOFC with micro gas turbine power generation systems. The self-evaluation of the present proposal is given. We believe that this proposal is of importance in academic values and has great potential for future industrial application. It is also a challenging research. The principle investigator (PI) has recently successfully established an atmospheric high-temperature SOFC testing platform for testing large-area full cells including flow distributors. The PI’s laboratory has established the standard test procedure for single-cell stacks under normal pressure conditions and thus accumulated the system integration experience for SOFC. Some achievements in terms of publications are listed below. 1. C. M. Huang, S. S. Shy and C. H. Lee (2008) “On Flow Uniformity in Various Interconnects and Its Influence to Cell Performance of Planar SOFC”, Journal of Power Sources, Vol. 183, No. 1, pp. 205-213. 2. C. M. Huang, S. S. Shy, C. W. Chien, C. H. Lee (2010) “Parametric Study of Anodic Microstructures to Cell Performance of Planar Solid Oxide Fuel Cells Using Measured Porous Transport Properties”, Journal of Power Sources, Vol. 195, pp. 2260-2265. 3. C. M. Huang, S. S. Shy, H.H. Li, C.H. Lee (2010) “The Impact of Flow Distributors on the Performance of Planar Solid Oxide Fuel Cell”, Journal of Power Sources, Vol. 195, pp. 6280-6286. (此篇為SOFC單電池堆實作測試論文，國際上仍屬少見) Supported by NSC continuously, the PI’s laboratory has established most of necessary facilities to perform the aforesaid measurements. Furthermore, the statistical methods for analyzing the measured data are also available. Thus, the possibility of completing this proposal should be very high. The PI and his post-doc and graduate students will all do their best to continuously produce important results with the highest quality in the international SOFC society.
items.completion:
The proposal outlines three main working items over the three years, each focused on advancing the development of high-pressure planar solid oxide fuel cells (SOFCs) for future clean energy systems. Here's the breakdown:

### **First Year (20208–20218):**
- **Objective 1:** Establish an innovative high-pressure (1–5 atm) SOFC testing platform. This includes conducting experiments to measure the impact of high pressure on the performance of single-cell stacks and developing a standard cell testing procedure under high-pressure conditions.
- **Objective 2:** Conduct initial experimental and analytical work to validate the performance of SOFC stacks under high-pressure conditions, laying the groundwork for future numerical modeling.

### **Second Year (20218–20228):**
- **Objective 1:** Develop 3D high-pressure electrochemical reaction flow numerical models. These models will simulate and predict the performance of SOFC stacks under high-pressure conditions, enabling comparison with experimental data.
- **Objective 2:** Continue the experimental work, focusing on refining the testing platform and ensuring data consistency.

### **Third Year (20228–20238):**
- **Objective 1:** Investigate micro-ion transport phenomena in single-cell stacks under high-pressure conditions, building on the numerical models developed in the second year.
- **Objective 2:** Combine experimental and numerical results to evaluate challenges and issues in integrating high-pressure planar SOFCs with micro-gas turbine power systems, ultimately aiming to establish a real-world testing platform for performance measurement.

### **Key Contributions and Support:**
- The proposal leverages the principal investigator’s (PI) existing infrastructure, including a successful atmospheric high-temperature SOFC testing platform and standard test procedures for single-cell stacks.
- Supported by continuous funding (NSC), the PI’s laboratory has established necessary facilities and statistical methods for data analysis, ensuring the feasibility of completing the proposal.

### **Conclusion:**
The three-year proposal aims to advance SOFC technology through experimental, numerical, and integration studies, with a focus on high-pressure testing and modeling. It aligns with global efforts to develop clean, efficient energy systems and is considered a challenging yet impactful research project.

items.prompt:
Can you summarize the history and implementation of quality control activities at Toyota Motor Co., Ltd. And Toyota Motor Sales Co., Ltd. After their merger? Answer according to: Quality control activities (sometimes abbreviated to "QC"), which were actively being pursed among dealers around the time of the merger of Toyota Motor Co., Ltd. And Toyota Motor Sales Co., Ltd., took off from around this same period as an approach to improving management systems.
The introduction of quality control to dealers, prompted by an awareness of the need for coordination between manufacturing and sales and for strengthening of sales capabilities, was decided at the end of 1980 at a joint policy meeting between Toyota Motor Co., Ltd. And Toyota Motor Sales Co., Ltd. In response, in February 1981, a an office to promote QC activities was set up within Toyota Motor Sales Co., Ltd. To start preparing for the introduction of quality control. In March 1982, the first executive study meeting for representatives of dealers nationwide was held, and quality control activities among dealers began.
By the beginning of 1983, the year following the merger of Toyota Motor Co., Ltd. And Toyota Motor Sales Co., Ltd., approximately 90 percent of all dealers, including parts distributors and others, had introduced quality control in some form. Responding to this increasingly widespread adoption of quality control, in February 1983 Toyota established the new QC Promotion Department responsible for promoting quality control Toyota-wide, and an award system was instituted under the title "Toyota Dealers QC Promotion Prize". Additionally, from 1984, the National QC Convention of Toyota Dealer was introduced as a forum for presenting the benefits of quality control and awarding prizes. As a result, by 1997, approximately 60 percent of dealers in Japan had received the Toyota Dealers QC Promotion Prize, a sign of the steady raising of levels of quality across the board.
The rollout to sales functions of quality control, which was seen at the time as a quality control method for manufacturing divisions, was an unusual step and attracted attention from many sectors of industry. The pursuit of quality, which is a founding principle of Toyota, was reaffirmed on the occasion of the merger of Toyota Motor Co., Ltd. And Toyota Motor Sales Co., Ltd, and brought major benefits to sales divisions also.
Meanwhile, along with the establishment of the TQC Promotion Department President Shoichiro Toyoda announced a policy for the management team to take the lead in tackling quality control. Accordingly, the 1st QC Study Group, attended by all board members, was held over three days and two nights in June 1983. The themes for discussion, which included "integration and increased efficiency in sales, product planning, development, and production" and "strengthening of dealer corporate systems", focused on internal coordination and coordination with dealer, as that was important immediately after the merger. From that year on, the QC training seminar for executives meeting came to be held every year, and in 1991, its ninth year, the group was renamed the Executive Study Group.
items.completion:
The history and implementation of quality control (QC) activities at Toyota Motor Co., Ltd. and Toyota Motor Sales Co., Ltd., following their merger, reflect a strategic initiative to enhance coordination between manufacturing and sales, strengthen dealer capabilities, and reinforce Toyota's foundational principle of quality. Here's a structured summary:

### **Origins and Initiation (1980–1981)**
- **1980**: A joint policy meeting between the two companies decided to introduce QC activities to dealers, aiming to improve management systems and enhance sales capabilities.  
- **1981**: A QC promotion office was established within Toyota Motor Sales Co., Ltd. to prepare for the introduction of QC.  
- **1982**: The first executive study meeting for dealers was held, marking the beginning of QC activities among dealers.

### **Widespread Adoption (1983–1997)**
- **1983**: By the start of 1983, over 90% of dealers, including parts distributors, had adopted QC in some form.  
- **1983**: Toyota established the **QC Promotion Department** to oversee Toyota-wide QC efforts and introduced the **Toyota Dealers QC Promotion Prize** as an award system.  
- **1984**: The **National QC Convention of Toyota Dealer** was introduced, serving as a platform to showcase QC benefits and award prizes.  
- **1997**: Approximately 60% of dealers in Japan had received the prize, indicating a steady rise in quality standards across the company.

### **Expansion to Sales Functions (1983–Present)**
- **1983**: QC was initially a manufacturing focus but was rolled out to sales functions as a strategic move to align manufacturing and sales.  
- **1983**: Toyota’s **TQC Promotion Department** was established, with President Shoichiro Toyoda emphasizing leadership in QC.  
- **1983**: The **1st QC Study Group** (attended by all board members) was held, focusing on internal coordination, dealer collaboration, and integrating QC into sales, product planning, and production.  
- **1991**: The group was renamed the **Executive Study Group**, highlighting its role in executive development.

### **Key Outcomes and Impact**
- **Quality as a Founding Principle**: The merger reinforced Toyota’s commitment to quality, benefiting not only manufacturing but also sales and dealer operations.  
- **Leadership and Training**: Executive training seminars became annual events, with the Executive Study Group evolving over time.  
- **Recognition and Culture**: The QC Promotion Prize and National Convention fostered a culture of excellence, encouraging dealers and employees to prioritize quality.

In summary, Toyota’s QC initiatives evolved from a dealer-focused approach to a comprehensive strategy that integrated quality into all aspects of the company, driven by the merger and a steadfast commitment to excellence.

items.prompt: Write a short story that revolves around a magical tree that can grant wishes to the travelers who pass by it, with attention given to character development, descriptive language, and a plot that showcases the consequences of making a wish.
items.completion:
In the quiet village of Elmsworth, nestled between misty hills and ancient woods, there stood a peculiar tree known as the Whispering Oak. Its branches stretched high, adorned with leaves that shimmered like embers, and its roots delved deep into the earth, whispering secrets to those who dared to listen. For centuries, the Oak had been a place of wonder, where travelers would come seeking fortune, love, or peace.

One winter, a young woman named Elara, weary of a life of solitude and loneliness, journeyed to Elmsworth. She had lost her family in a tragic accident and had spent years searching for meaning. As she approached the Oak, the air grew heavy with the scent of pine and forgotten memories. The tree stood motionless, its branches swaying gently in the wind, as if waiting.

Elara approached the Oak, her heart racing with hope. She reached out, and the tree responded. A voice, both gentle and ancient, echoed in her mind: “If you wish for something, you must first understand the cost.”

With a deep breath, Elara whispered, “I wish for love.” The Oak shuddered, and a soft light enveloped her. In an instant, she found herself standing before a man she had never met—her long-lost brother, Alex. Their eyes met, and a warmth surged through her heart. She felt the joy of reunion, the comfort of shared memories.

But as the days passed, the weight of her wish began to settle in. Elara found herself longing for more than just love. She wished for a life filled with adventure, for the thrill of new beginnings, and for the freedom to explore the world beyond Elmsworth. The Oak, now more attuned to her desires, responded with a surge of energy. The world around her shimmered, and she felt herself being pulled into a new realm.

In that moment, she realized the true cost of her wish. The world she had once known was now a shadow of what it once was. The people she loved were no longer as they once were, and the memories she cherished were fading. She had traded one dream for another, and the consequences were beginning to unravel her sense of self.

As the seasons changed, Elara returned to the Oak, her heart heavy. The tree, once a source of hope, now stood as a reminder of the price of unchecked wishes. She understood that true fulfillment lay not in the granting of wishes, but in the wisdom to know when to let go.

And so, Elara left Elmsworth, carrying the lessons of the Whispering Oak with her, knowing that sometimes, the greatest magic comes not from what you wish for, but from what you choose to keep.
